narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection notice for our program. The admissions model we use evaluates your likelihood of passing the bar exam on your first try, based on key aspects of your academic history and profile. For you specifically, it predicted a 96% chance of failing the bar exam, which is why your application was denied—our goal is to admit students with the strongest predicted odds of bar success to ensure their long-term achievement. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile and signaled weaker performance in those later years. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile of 2nd—again well below the average around 6th—further reinforced concerns about your early academic standing relative to peers. Your LSAT score of 28 also played a significant role, as it's substantially lower than the dataset average of about 37, pulling the prediction more toward failure. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.3, compared to the typical 3.2, contributed in the same direction, though to a lesser degree. Your middle family income bracket had a modest negative influence as well, aligning with patterns seen in the data. The only factor nudging slightly toward success was your choice of full-time enrollment, which is the norm for 93% of students and thus didn't stand out positively. Overall, your profile stands out as lower than typical applicants across these core academic metrics, which drove the high failure risk. While this decision is tough, we encourage you to focus on strengthening your grades, retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and building a stronger academic record—perhaps through additional coursework or test prep—before reapplying to law programs. You're capable of growth, and many students in similar situations have turned things around with targeted effort. We wish you the best in your journey ahead.
